ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Mulligatawny code exists in FoodEx2.

main_ingredients: red lentils (masoor dal) or yellow lentils (toor dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mulligatawny soup is approximately 250 g (one bowl), as typically served in South Indian and Anglo-Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mulligatawny Soup (vegetarian lentil-based) — estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods: Masoor Dal (red lentil soup) and component ingredient data
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition), Hyderabad, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Mulligatawny soup exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) using the closest analogous entry: cooked red lentil (masoor dal) soup with spices and vegetables. The dish is a dilute spiced lentil broth; per-100g values reflect approximately 8–10% cooked lentil solids in a spiced broth base. Energy and macros are consistent with published values for thin lentil soups in the USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy: Soup, lentil, canned, ready-to-serve, NDB 06215) used as a cross-reference validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Lentil soup and lentil-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mulligatawny soup. GI of 30 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for lentil soup (GI approximately 30, low category), which is the closest analogous food. Mulligatawny is a spiced lentil broth with similar lentil content and preparation method. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 14.5) / 100 = 4.35, rounded to 4.4. Serving carbohydrate = 5.8 g per 100g × 250g serving = 14.5g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 280 mg per 100g; a 250g serving delivers approximately 700 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a standard 250g bowl of Mulligatawny soup contributes approximately 700 mg sodium (about 30% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~30) due to lentil base; high dietary fibre and protein slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mulligatawny soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (~30), moderate protein (2.4g/100g), and dietary fibre (1.4g/100g) from lentils, which collectively attenuate postprandial blood glucose rise; a standard 250g serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 4.4, which is low.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and lentil-derived soluble fibre support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetarian Mulligatawny soup is cardioprotective in profile — it contains no cholesterol, low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), and lentil-derived soluble fibre that may help reduce LDL cholesterol; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mulligatawny soup (vegetarian) is a classic South Indian and Anglo-Indian spiced lentil broth with deep historical roots in Tamil Nadu cuisine. The name derives from the Tamil words 'milagu' (pepper) and 'thanneer' (water), reflecting its origins as a peppery thin broth. The vegetarian version is made by simmering red or yellow lentils (masoor or toor dal) with onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, black pepper, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and curry leaves, then finishing with a tempering of whole spices in oil. The result is a thin, golden-orange soup with a warm, mildly spicy, slightly tangy flavour profile and a smooth, lightly textured consistency. In Singapore, it is commonly served in North and South Indian restaurants as a starter or accompaniment to rice or bread. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-GI food (GI ~30) providing moderate plant protein and dietary fibre from lentils, with negligible saturated fat and no cholesterol. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The sodium content from added salt and spices is moderate and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free in its traditional preparation.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Mulligatawny soup (vegetarian) exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN cooked lentil data and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy lentil soup data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous lentil soup entry. Confidence is medium due to the estimation basis.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from input 'Mulligtwanny Shorba-Veg' to canonical English 'Mulligatawny Soup (Vegetarian)'; original spelling preserved in aliases. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN (2017) cooked red lentil data and USDA SR Legacy lentil soup (NDB 06215) as cross-reference; no exact Mulligatawny entry exists in approved sources. 3. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database lentil soup entry; no direct GI measurement for Mulligatawny soup was found in approved sources. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this plant-based preparation with no hydrogenated fats. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based vegetarian dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish; B12 is not naturally present in plant foods. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present in this plant-based soup. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 9. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in a Singapore restaurant context. 10. The dish is classified as South Indian / Anglo-Indian cuisine; the restaurant context is North Indian (Aryana), but the dish itself is of South Indian origin. 11. is_vegan set to true as the vegetarian version uses oil rather than ghee; if ghee is used, is_vegan would be false — this should be confirmed with the restaurant.
